title: From Fate to Dharma
subtitle: the journey home
goalMeaning: Every piece is a soul; the Charkoni is fulfillment. To bring all four home is to complete the journey from fate to dharma.

enter.text: A soul steps from the still centre onto the winding path. The journey has begun.
Every life sets out from the same quiet source. Walk it well.
The cowries fall as fate; the choice of which piece to move is yours. Here fate meets will.
To be born is to be given a road. Dharma is how you walk it.

castle.text: A castle square — a refuge. Here no one can touch you; pause, breathe, and gather your resolve.
Even the traveller must rest. Stillness on the path is not delay but strength.
Safe ground is earned, not given. Hold it, and let the storm of fate pass by.
The wise pause at the crossing four-and-twenty steps from home, and wait for their moment.

capture.text: The wheel of fortune turns — an opponent is sent home to begin again. Take no pride; the wheel turns for all.
In the great dice-game of the Mahabharata, one throw changed the fate of kingdoms. So does yours.
Victory here is only a moment. Act without attachment to its fruit.
You have unseated another soul. Karma remembers both the deed and the spirit behind it.

captured.text: Your soul is sent back to the Charkoni. All that was gained returns to nothing — and yet you rise again.
The fall is not the end; it is the invitation to begin more wisely.
Loss visits everyone on the board. Meet it without bitterness and the road stays open.

home.text: A soul completes its circuit and comes home. The long road from fate to dharma is fulfilled.
It went out into the world and returned changed. This is the whole of a life.
One piece rests in the centre now, free of the wheel. Three journeys remain.

win.text: All four souls are home. The player has walked the whole journey — from the throw of fate to the peace of dharma.
The circle closes where it began, in the still centre. This is the victory the ancients called liberation.

journey.text: The soul reaches the tip of its own arm and turns outward into the wide world.
You cross into a new quarter of the board. Every stage of life has its own weather.
Halfway round. The going-out is done; from here, all roads bend toward home.
The far country lies behind you. What you carry now is only what you have become.
Your own arm rises ahead. The homeward climb up the centre begins.
